records.ety: [L. institutus, p. p. of instituere to place in, to institute, to instruct; pref. in- in + statuere to cause to stand, to set. See Statute.]

quotes: They have but few laws. For to a people so instruct and institute, very few to suffice.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Instituted (n"st*tū`td); p. pr. & vb. n. Instituting.]

defs: To set up; to establish; to ordain; as, to institute laws, rules, etc.
To originate and establish; to found; to organize; as, to institute a court, or a society.
quotes: Whenever any from of government becomes destructive of these ends it is the right of the people to alter or to abolish it, and to institute a new government.
We institute your Grace To be our regent in these parts of France.

defs: To begin; to commence; to set on foot; as, to institute an inquiry; to institute a suit.
quotes: And haply institute A course of learning and ingenious studies.

defs: To ground or establish in principles and rudiments; to educate; to instruct.
quotes: If children were early instituted, knowledge would insensibly insinuate itself.

defs: To invest with the spiritual charge of a benefice, or the care of souls.

records.ety: [L. institutum: cf. F. institut. See Institute, v. t. & a.]

defs: That which is instituted, established, or fixed, as a law, habit, or custom.

Hence: An elementary and necessary principle; a precept, maxim, or rule, recognized as established and authoritative; usually in the plural, a collection of such principles and precepts; esp., a comprehensive summary of legal principles and decisions; as, the Institutes of Justinian; Coke's Institutes of the Laws of England. Cf. Digest, n.
quotes: They made a sort of institute and digest of anarchy.
To make the Stoics' institutes thy own.

defs: An institution; a society established for the promotion of learning, art, science, etc.; a college; as, the Institute of Technology; The Massachusetts Institute of Technology; also, a building owned or occupied by such an institute; as, the Cooper Institute.
The person to whom an estate is first given by destination or limitation.
